prompt: Develop a Workforce & Talent Operations Hub system for offline-first organizations that manage recruiting pipelines, collector dispatch, and internal analytics from a single on-premise web portal. The system serves four roles: Administrator (configures master data, security, and metric definitions), Recruiter/Staffing Manager (searches talent and manages pipelines), Dispatch Supervisor (plans shifts and dispatch policies), and Finance Clerk (records payments and issues refunds). Using Thymeleaf-rendered pages, users sign in with a local username and password and land on a role-based dashboard showing today’s open requisitions, unassigned jobs, and exception alerts. Recruiters run intelligent filtering and match searches across structured candidate profiles and job profiles using multi-criteria filters with Boolean logic and weighted ranking; results show an explainable rationale highlighting matched skills and required years of experience (for example, “Java 5+ years met; Spring Boot preferred met”). Users can save searches, group candidates into talent pools, bulk tag, and move batches through pipeline stages with a single confirmation step and an undo option valid for 60 seconds. Dispatch Supervisors maintain collector and site profiles, define work shifts in 15-minute increments, and set capacity limits per site; dispatch can run in grab-order mode (collectors accept) or assigned-order mode (system assigns), with a 90-second acceptance timeout followed by auto-redispatch. Business users rely on unified search to find members, enterprises, resources, orders, and redemption records with combined filters, saved criteria, and export controls; exports run as queued jobs and appear in an “Exports” page with status and download links. On the system side, the backend uses Spring Boot to expose REST-style endpoints consumed by the Thymeleaf UI, enforcing server-side validation, authorization, and consistent audit logging. MySQL persists master data (students/classes/departments/courses/semesters adapted as organizational training and staffing reference tables), with uniqueness and referential integrity enforced; bulk import/export supports Excel/CSV with strict format checks, row-level error reports, and a local file fingerprint to prevent accidental re-import of the same file. Intelligent matching and similarity scoring are computed locally and stored with the search snapshot so “why matched” remains stable even if profiles change. A Python-based local recognition service runs on the same network segment to perform face-feature extraction and matching for optional identity checks; Spring Boot applies rate limits (for example, 30 requests/minute per user) and submits heavy work to an internal async job queue for feature computation, batch imports, and report generation, with health checks and configurable thresholds. The metrics framework is managed in a semantic layer that stores metric/dimension definitions, derived metrics, window calculations, versioning, and lineage so charts remain consistent across releases; only Administrators can publish a new metric version, and rollbacks must be supported. Payments and settlement are handled entirely offline as recorded transactions across allowed channels (cash, check, and manual card entry without network processing), with idempotent callback handling for imported bank files, full/partial refunds, reconciliation with exception queues, and monthly settlement statements exported as CSV. Sensitive data is stored locally in MySQL with field-level masking in the UI (for example, only last 4 digits shown) and encryption at rest for biometric templates; all access requires role permissions and is auditable by timestamp and workstation ID.
